summary: Gruber analyzes Mark Papermaster's departure from Apple following the iPhone 4 antenna controversy. He argues the antenna is fundamentally a good design with a single flaw — signal loss when touched in a certain spot — rather than a wholesale failure. He notes circumstantial evidence that Papermaster may have been on the outs before Antennagate, pointing to Bob Mansfield appearing in the iPhone 4 promo video covering Papermaster's areas of expertise. Gruber reveals that the antenna touch-sensitivity bug was filed two years prior, meaning Apple knew about the problem but failed to solve it in time. He predicts the next iPhone will keep the external antenna design while fixing the flaw, rather than retreating to an internal antenna.
key_points: The iPhone 4 antenna is a good design with one specific flaw — signal loss from skin contact at a certain spot — not a fundamentally broken product
Papermaster's absence from the iPhone 4 promotional video, with Mansfield covering his areas of expertise, suggests he may have been sidelined before the antenna controversy
The antenna bug was filed two years before launch, meaning Apple knowingly shipped a known issue rather than missing it entirely
Apple's one-iPhone-per-year strategy makes hardware mistakes uniquely high-stakes compared to competitors with broader product lines
The external antenna's benefits — stronger signal, space savings for a larger battery, smaller form factor — make it likely Apple will iterate on the design rather than abandon it
money_quotes: Here's the iPhone 4 antenna, in eight words: It's a good design but has a flaw. The problem is the last four words.
Does Apple have a 'make one mistake and you're fired' policy? No. But, if the mistake is big enough, sometimes yes.

Keep in mind that Apple's product strategy is riskier than that of other companies. They don't have a line-up of iPhones. They don't roll new models out every three or four months. They do one new iPhone per year. It's high stakes, and high pressure.
The bug on the 'touching it wrong' signal loss issue was filed two years ago. This is not a problem they didn't catch, or caught too late.
It's not that Apple should not have used an external antenna. It's that it should have been even better.
key_insight: The iPhone 4 antenna was a calculated design risk that Apple knowingly shipped with a known flaw, and the real failure was not solving a two-year-old bug before launch.
